title: Finite-Width Phase Diagrams for Neural Network Training Dynamics via Lyapunov Stability of the NTK Trajectory
summary: We develop a theoretical and computational framework for constructing phase diagrams that delineate lazy (kernel) and rich (feature-learning) regimes of neural network training at finite width. The approach replaces the classical steady-state bifurcation analysis with a variational Lyapunov stability analysis of the frozen-kernel (NTK) training trajectory, yielding a well-defined stability boundary in terms of the finite-time Lyapunov exponent. A 1/N perturbative expansion of the NTK — with novel H-tensor decompositions for convolutional and residual architectures — drives a coupled kernel-residual ODE whose solutions predict the phase boundary location, scaling exponents, and universality classes. The system uses the Kernel Alignment Drift Rate (KADR) as a unified operational order parameter, validated against multi-indicator ground truth, and is computationally feasible on a single laptop for architectures up to depth 6 and width 1024.

definitions.name: Definition 1.1: Parameterized Network
definitions.formal_statement: Let f_θ: ℝ^d → ℝ^c be an L-layer neural network with parameter vector θ ∈ ℝ^P, width N_ℓ at layer ℓ, and nonlinearity σ. The dataset is D = {(x_i, y_i)}_{i=1}^n with x_i ∈ ℝ^d, y_i ∈ ℝ^c.
definitions.purpose: Establishes the basic network object and notation used throughout all theorems and algorithms

definitions.name: Definition 1.2: Neural Tangent Kernel
definitions.formal_statement: The empirical NTK at parameters θ is Θ_{ij}(θ) = Σ_p (∂f(x_i;θ)/∂θ_p)(∂f(x_j;θ)/∂θ_p) = J(θ)J(θ)^T where J is the n×P Jacobian matrix.
definitions.purpose: Defines the central object whose dynamics we track; the NTK governs training dynamics in the linearized regime and its drift characterizes the lazy-to-rich transition

definitions.name: Definition 1.3: μP Scaling Exponents
definitions.formal_statement: A parameterization P assigns to each layer ℓ exponents (a_ℓ, b_ℓ, c_ℓ) such that: initialization scale σ_ℓ² = N_ℓ^{-a_ℓ}, learning rate multiplier η_ℓ = N_ℓ^{-b_ℓ}, output scale α = N^{-c}. Standard parameterization: a=1, b=0, c=0. Maximal update (μP): a=1, b=1, c=1/2.
definitions.purpose: Parameterization determines the effective coupling strength γ and hence the phase boundary location; different parameterizations yield different phase diagrams

definitions.name: Definition 1.4: Finite-Width NTK Expansion
definitions.formal_statement: Under Gaussian initialization at width N, the NTK admits the expansion Θ(t) = Θ^{(0)}(t) + (1/N)Θ^{(1)}(t) + O(1/N²), where Θ^{(0)} is the infinite-width (deterministic) kernel and Θ^{(1)}(t) is the first-order finite-width correction satisfying the kernel evolution ODE (Definition 1.6*).
definitions.purpose: Provides the perturbative structure that makes finite-width predictions tractable; the 1/N correction is the minimal ingredient needed to capture kernel drift and hence the phase transition

definitions.name: Definition 1.5: Residual Vector
definitions.formal_statement: The training residual is r_i(t) = f(x_i; θ(t)) - y_i. Under gradient flow with squared loss: dr/dt = -η Θ(t) r(t).
definitions.purpose: The residual drives kernel evolution through the H-tensor coupling; its magnitude determines whether the system is in the lazy or rich regime

definitions.name: Definition 1.6*: Kernel Evolution ODE (Frozen-H)
definitions.formal_statement: The leading finite-width correction to the NTK evolves as dΘ^{(1)}_{ij}/dt = -(η/N) Σ_k r_k(t) H_{ijk} where H_{ijk} is the Hessian-Jacobian contraction tensor evaluated at initialization and held frozen (H = H(t=0)). The full coupled system is: dr/dt = -η[Θ^{(0)} + (1/N)Θ^{(1)}(t)]r(t), dΘ^{(1)}_{ij}/dt = -(η/N) Σ_k r_k(t) H_{ijk}(0).
definitions.purpose: Defines the dynamical system whose solutions predict kernel drift and phase boundaries; the frozen-H approximation is justified by the structural stability lemma and validated empirically

definitions.name: Definition 1.7a: H Tensor for MLPs
definitions.formal_statement: For an L-layer MLP with pre-activations h^{(ℓ)} = W^{(ℓ)}σ(h^{(ℓ-1)}), the Hessian-Jacobian contraction tensor decomposes as H_{ijk} = Σ_ℓ Σ_{α,β} (∂²f_i/∂W^{(ℓ)}_{αβ}∂W^{(ℓ')}_{α'β'}) · (∂f_j/∂W^{(ℓ)}_{αβ}) · (∂f_k/∂W^{(ℓ')}_{α'β'}). Under Gaussian moment closure, this simplifies to layer-wise contributions involving σ'' and cross-layer kernel products.
definitions.purpose: Provides the analytic form of H for the simplest architecture class, serving as the base case for more complex architectures

definitions.name: Definition 1.7b: H Tensor for Convolutional Layers (Novel)
definitions.formal_statement: For a convolutional layer with filter W ∈ ℝ^{C_out × C_in × k_h × k_w} and spatial output locations indexed by α ∈ S (the spatial grid), weight sharing induces: H^{conv}_{ijk} = (1/|S|) Σ_{α∈S} H^{dense}_{ijk}(α) + O(ξ/√|S|), where H^{dense}_{ijk}(α) is the fully-connected H tensor evaluated at spatial location α, and ξ is the spatial correlation length of the data.
definitions.purpose: Extends the H-tensor framework to convolutional architectures; the spatial averaging factor 1/|S| implies that ConvNets have effectively larger width, shifting the phase boundary

definitions.name: Definition 1.7c: H Tensor for Residual Connections (Novel)
definitions.formal_statement: For a residual block h^{(ℓ+1)} = h^{(ℓ)} + g^{(ℓ)}(h^{(ℓ)}) where g^{(ℓ)} is the residual branch: H^{res}_{ijk} = H^{branch}_{ijk} + Ξ_ℓ where Ξ_ℓ is a cross-term between the skip and branch paths involving ∂g/∂h · ∂²g/∂W². The depth scaling satisfies ||H^{res}||/||H^{dense}|| = O(1/L) per block, leading to cumulative H ~ O(1) across the full network.
definitions.purpose: Enables phase diagram prediction for ResNet-style architectures; the O(1/L) per-block scaling explains why deeper ResNets maintain a similar phase boundary

definitions.name: Definition 1.8: Dimensionless Coupling Parameter
definitions.formal_statement: For a network with μP exponents (a, b) and uniform width N: γ = η · N^{-(1-a-b)} · ||H||_F / ||Θ^{(0)}||_F. Standard parameterization (a=1, b=0): γ = η · ||H||_F / ||Θ^{(0)}||_F (independent of N). μP (a=1, b=1): γ = η · N · ||H||_F / ||Θ^{(0)}||_F (grows with N).
definitions.purpose: γ is the single dimensionless number that controls the lazy-to-rich transition; the phase boundary is at γ = γ_c, and all phase diagrams are parameterized by γ

definitions.name: Definition 1.9: Order Parameter — Kernel Alignment Drift Rate (KADR)
definitions.formal_statement: The kernel alignment drift rate at time T is ρ(T) = ||Θ(T) - Θ(0)||_F / ||Θ(0)||_F. The time-averaged KADR is ρ̄ = (1/T) ∫_0^T ρ(t) dt. The normalized KADR across seeds is ρ̄ = (1/S) Σ_s ρ̄^{(s)}.
definitions.purpose: KADR is the primary order parameter distinguishing lazy (ρ̄ ≈ 0) from rich (ρ̄ ≫ 0) regimes; it is computable from both theoretical ODE trajectories and empirical training runs, enabling direct theory-experiment comparison

definitions.name: Definition 1.10*: Phase Boundary via Trajectory Stability (Corrected)
definitions.formal_statement: Let r_0(t) be the NTK trajectory solving dr_0/dt = -η Θ^{(0)} r_0(t) with r_0(0) = f_{θ_0}(x) - y. Define the variational system for perturbations (δΘ, δr) around this trajectory: d(δr_i)/dt = -η Σ_j Θ^{(0)}_{ij} δr_j - η Σ_j δΘ_{ij} · r_{0,j}(t), d(δΘ_{ij})/dt = -η Σ_k r_{0,k}(t) · H_{ijk}. This is a non-autonomous linear system. Define the finite-time Lyapunov exponent: Λ_T(γ) = (1/T) ln(sup_{||v_0||=1} ||Φ(T,0)v_0||) where Φ(T,0) is the state-transition matrix. The phase boundary is Γ = {(γ, L, D) : Λ_T(γ, L, D) = 0}. Λ_T < 0 → lazy regime (perturbations decay), Λ_T > 0 → rich regime (perturbations grow).
definitions.purpose: This is the corrected theoretical definition of the phase boundary, replacing the original steady-state bifurcation analysis which had a block-triangular Jacobian with no feedback. The variational system has full two-way coupling: δΘ feeds back into δr via the r_0(t) term, and δr drives δΘ through H. The Lyapunov exponent crosses zero as γ increases, yielding a well-defined stability boundary.

definitions.name: Definition 1.10a: Phase Boundary as Steepest-Gradient Locus (Primary Operational Definition)
definitions.formal_statement: In the continuous phase diagram over parameters p = (γ, L, κ_data), the phase boundary is the steepest-gradient locus: γ_c = argmax_γ ||∇_γ ρ̄(γ)||, where ρ̄(γ) is the KADR evaluated from the kernel ODE trajectory at coupling γ.
definitions.purpose: This is the PRIMARY operational definition used for both theoretical predictions and experimental measurements. It is robust to finite-N smearing, well-defined even when the transition is a crossover rather than a sharp bifurcation, and computable from both ODE solutions and training runs.
dependencies: Definition 1.9: Order Parameter — Kernel Alignment Drift Rate (KADR)

definitions.name: Definition 1.12: Perturbative Validity Indicators
definitions.formal_statement: A prediction at parameters (γ, N, L) is assessed by: ε_1 = ||Θ^{(1)}||_F / (N · ||Θ^{(0)}||_F) (perturbative ratio), ε_2 = fit residual from WLS regression, ε_3 = |γ_c^{1-loop} - γ_c^{2-loop}| / γ_c^{1-loop} (two-loop convergence), ε_4 = Nyström relative error (if applicable), κ(Θ^{(0)}) = condition number. Confidence tiers: HIGH if ε_1 < 0.2, ε_2 < 0.1, ε_3 < 0.15, κ < 10^6; MODERATE if any one indicator marginally exceeded; LOW if any critical threshold exceeded.
definitions.purpose: Self-diagnostic validity indicators that flag when predictions are unreliable, providing calibrated uncertainty without requiring ground truth

definitions.name: Definition 1.13: Gaussian Moment Closure
definitions.formal_statement: The perturbative hierarchy at order 1/N requires the 4th cumulant κ_4 of the pre-activation distribution. The Gaussian closure approximation sets κ_4 = 0, truncating the cumulant hierarchy. This is exact at initialization for Gaussian weights and remains approximately valid for C³ activations (GELU, Softplus, Swish).
definitions.purpose: Closes the perturbative hierarchy at finite order, making the kernel evolution ODE tractable; without this closure, the 1/N equation for Θ^{(1)} couples to κ_4, which couples to κ_6, creating an infinite hierarchy

definitions.name: Definition 1.13a: Moment Closure Sensitivity
definitions.formal_statement: The moment-closure sensitivity coefficient is χ_{κ_4} = ∂γ_c/∂κ_4 |_{κ_4=0}, estimated by perturbing the H tensor: H̃_{ijk} = H_{ijk} + δκ_4 · ΔH_{ijk} and measuring the shift in γ_c.
definitions.purpose: Quantifies the systematic bias introduced by the Gaussian closure; if χ_{κ_4} is large, the moment closure significantly affects the phase boundary prediction

definitions.name: Definition 1.14: Nyström-Approximated Kernel
definitions.formal_statement: Given n data points and m ≪ n landmark points, the rank-m Nyström approximation is Θ̃ = K_{nm} K_{mm}^{-1} K_{mn}, stored in factored form L = K_{nm} U[:,:r] diag(1/√Σ[:r]) so that Θ̃ = LL^T.
definitions.purpose: Reduces the O(n²) kernel storage and O(n³) solve to O(nm) and O(nr²), enabling scaling to large datasets; only used for n > 1000 (primary experiments use n_probe = 200 with exact kernels)

definitions.name: Definition 1.15: Pseudo-Arclength Continuation
definitions.formal_statement: Let F(γ, p) = Λ_T(γ, p) = 0 define the phase boundary implicitly, where p collects non-coupling parameters (depth L, data complexity, etc.). Given a solution (γ_0, p_0) ∈ Γ and tangent vector (γ̇_0, ṗ_0), the next solution (γ_1, p_1) is found by solving the augmented system: F(γ_1, p_1) = 0, τ_prev · (γ_1 - γ_pred, p_1 - p_pred) = 0 (arclength constraint).
definitions.purpose: Enables efficient tracing of phase boundary curves through multi-dimensional parameter space once an initial boundary point is found, avoiding the need to re-run bisection at every parameter value
dependencies: Definition 1.10*: Phase Boundary via Trajectory Stability (Corrected)

assumptions.statement: Gaussian initialization. Weights are i.i.d. N(0, σ_ℓ²/N_ℓ) with Kaiming/He scaling.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: The 1/N expansion structure (Def. 1.4) and cumulant hierarchy both assume Gaussian initial measure. Non-Gaussian initialization (e.g., uniform, orthogonal) would require different expansion coefficients and potentially different H-tensor formulas.

assumptions.statement: Gradient flow. Training dynamics are modeled as continuous-time gradient flow dθ/dt = -η∇L(θ), an O(η²) approximation to discrete gradient descent.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: At large learning rates (η > 0.1), discretization error accumulates and the gradient flow ODE predictions diverge from actual training. The phase boundary may shift by O(η²). Edge-of-stability phenomena at large η are not captured.

assumptions.statement: Squared loss. L = (1/2)Σ_i ||f(x_i) - y_i||². Classification is handled via one-hot regression.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: The residual evolution dr/dt = -ηΘr takes a clean linear form only for squared loss. Cross-entropy or other losses introduce nonlinear r-dependence in the evolution equation, breaking the ODE structure.

assumptions.statement: Uniform width. All hidden layers have width N. For ConvNets, use N_eff = min_ℓ N_ℓ as the effective width.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: With non-uniform widths, different layers contribute at different orders in the 1/N expansion. The expansion parameter becomes 1/N_eff = 1/min_ℓ N_ℓ, and the phase boundary prediction quality degrades for highly non-uniform architectures (max_ℓ N_ℓ / min_ℓ N_ℓ > 4).

assumptions.statement: 1/N expansion convergence. The one-loop truncation captures qualitative phase structure. Verified empirically via the two-loop check ε_3.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: If the expansion is only asymptotic and diverges at practical widths, the ODE-based predictions are unreliable. The minimum reliable width may be too large (N > 512) for practical relevance. Monitored via ε_3 < 0.15.

assumptions.statement: Gaussian moment closure. The 4th cumulant κ_4 of pre-activations is set to zero. Well-justified for GELU (κ_4 small); poor for ReLU (κ_4 ≠ 0).
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: Non-zero κ_4 shifts the effective H tensor and hence the phase boundary by O(κ_4/N). Monitored via κ_4/κ_2² at initialization; flagged if > 0.3.

assumptions.statement: Layer-wise independence at initialization. Pre-activations are independent across neurons within each layer. No BatchNorm, no weight correlations at initialization.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: BatchNorm or LayerNorm couples neurons within a layer, violating the independence needed for the cumulant decomposition that yields per-layer H contributions. The entire perturbative framework breaks down for normalized architectures.

assumptions.statement: C³ activation function with bounded derivatives: |σ^{(k)}| ≤ C_k for k = 0, 1, 2, 3. Satisfied by GELU, Softplus, Swish, tanh, sigmoid. ReLU is explicitly EXCLUDED from theorem statements (see Conjecture 1).
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: ReLU has a discontinuous second derivative; the H-tensor decomposition requires σ'' to be bounded. Without C³ bounds, the perturbative error bounds (Theorem 5*) become vacuous. ReLU is handled as Conjecture 1 with empirical support.

assumptions.statement: Data regularity. The input Gram matrix X^TX/d is well-conditioned with no duplicate or near-duplicate inputs. Verified by checking κ(X^TX/d) before running.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: Ill-conditioned data leads to near-singular NTK, amplifying numerical errors in the ODE integration and eigenvalue computation. Degenerate inputs collapse the effective sample size.

assumptions.statement: Finite training horizon. Predictions are for t ∈ [0, T] with T = 2000 gradient steps (primary) or T = 10000 (extended). The Grönwall bound is exponential in T.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: At very large T, the perturbative expansion accumulates error exponentially (Theorem 5*). The frozen-H approximation also degrades as the kernel drifts further from initialization.

assumptions.statement: Scalar output (c = 1). Multi-class handled via one-vs-all (10 independent scalar problems for MNIST/CIFAR). NTK is block-diagonal; theory applies per output.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: With coupled multi-output, the NTK becomes a block matrix and the H-tensor gains additional indices. The framework extends naturally but computational cost scales with c².

assumptions.statement: Full-batch gradient flow. No mini-batch noise. Use full-batch training with batch size = n_train in all experiments.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: Mini-batch SGD introduces gradient noise that can regularize training and shift the phase boundary. The noise scale depends on batch size, creating an additional parameter axis not modeled by the theory.

assumptions.statement: No bias terms. Biases initialized to zero. Biases contribute O(1/N) to the NTK, consistent with the perturbative framework.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: Non-zero biases break the symmetry of the initialization distribution but their NTK contribution is subdominant at large N.

assumptions.statement: No BatchNorm, LayerNorm, or other normalization layers. These couple neurons within a layer, violating A7.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: Normalization layers fundamentally change the NTK structure and invalidate the perturbative expansion.

assumptions.statement: Probe set size n_probe = 200. All NTK computations (both system predictions and ground truth) use 200 probe points. At n = 200, exact kernel computation is tractable; Nyström is unnecessary.
assumptions.consequences_if_violated: Smaller n_probe may miss fine-grained kernel structure; larger n_probe increases computation cost quadratically.

theorems.name: Theorem 1*: 1/N Expansion Structure for Weight-Shared Architectures (Smooth Activations)
theorems.statement: Under Assumptions A1, A4, A7, A8*, for a weight-shared architecture (convolutional or residual) with uniform width N and C³ activation σ, the empirical NTK admits the expansion Θ_{ij}(t) = Θ^{(0)}_{ij}(t) + (1/N)Θ^{(1)}_{ij}(t) + O(1/N²) uniformly in t ∈ [0, T], where: (1) Θ^{(0)} is the deterministic infinite-width kernel, (2) Θ^{(1)}(t) satisfies the kernel evolution ODE (Def. 1.6*) with H_{ijk} given by Definition 1.7b for convolutional layers.
theorems.proof_strategy: Extend the Dyer & Gur-Ari (2020) MLP proof to weight-shared architectures. The key new ingredient is tracking how weight sharing affects the cumulant decomposition: shared weights reduce the number of independent random variables, changing the combinatorial structure of the 1/N expansion. Use the layer-wise independence assumption (A7) and Gaussian moment closure to close the hierarchy. The C³ bound on σ (A8*) provides uniform control on the Taylor remainder.
theorems.enables: Extending the ODE-based prediction system from MLPs to ConvNets and ResNets. Without this theorem, the system is restricted to MLPs only.

theorems.name: Theorem 2*: H_{ijk} Decomposition for Convolutional Layers (Smooth Activations)
theorems.statement: Under Assumptions A1, A4, A7, A8*, for a convolutional layer with C_out filters of size k_h × k_w and spatial grid S, the H tensor decomposes as H^{conv}_{ijk} = (1/|S|) Σ_{α∈S} H^{dense}_{ijk}(α) + R_{ijk} where ||R||_F ≤ C · ξ/√|S| · ||H^{dense}||_F, with ξ the spatial correlation length of the data, and H^{dense}_{ijk}(α) is the fully-connected H tensor evaluated at spatial location α.
theorems.proof_strategy: Write the convolutional parameter Jacobian as a sum over spatial positions (each shared weight contributes at multiple locations). The H tensor inherits this summation structure. Apply the CLT-like spatial averaging argument: since the spatial contributions are only weakly correlated (correlation decaying with distance on the grid), the sum concentrates around its mean with fluctuations of order 1/√|S|. Lemma 3 provides the bound on the correction term R in terms of the spatial correlation length ξ.
theorems.enables: Analytic H_{ijk} for ConvNets, avoiding expensive numerical Hessian computation. Enables the spatial averaging prediction: ConvNets have effectively larger width N_eff ~ N·|S|.

theorems.name: Theorem 3*: H_{ijk} Composition Rule for Skip Connections (Smooth Activations)
theorems.statement: Under Assumptions A1, A4, A7, A8*, for a residual block h^{(ℓ+1)} = h^{(ℓ)} + g^{(ℓ)}(h^{(ℓ)}): (1) H^{res}_{ijk} = H^{branch}_{ijk} + Ξ_ℓ where Ξ_ℓ is the skip-branch cross-term. (2) ||H^{res}_ℓ||_F / ||H^{dense}_ℓ||_F = O(1/L) per residual block. (3) For L residual blocks: ||Σ_ℓ H^{res}_ℓ||_F = O(1), independent of depth L. (4) Depth-scaling corollary: γ_c(L) ~ L^δ with δ > 0 computable from the cross-term structure.
theorems.proof_strategy: Decompose the full-network Hessian into per-block contributions using the chain rule through skip connections. The skip path provides a direct gradient pathway that does not involve σ'', while the branch path contributes H^{branch}. The cross-term Ξ_ℓ arises from mixed second derivatives. The 1/L scaling per block follows from the μP-compatible initialization where branch outputs are scaled by 1/√L. Sum over L blocks to get O(1) total, establishing that the phase boundary exists even in the infinite-depth limit.
theorems.enables: Phase diagram prediction for ResNet-style architectures and depth-scaling analysis. Enables the prediction that γ_c grows with depth as L^δ.

theorems.name: Theorem 4*: Stability Boundary Characterization (Corrected)
theorems.statement: Consider the variational system (Definition 1.10*) with uniform width N and dimensionless coupling γ (Definition 1.8). Under Assumptions A1–A6, A8*, and the technical condition Θ^{(0)} ≻ 0: (1) Existence of critical coupling: There exists γ_c > 0 such that Λ_T(γ) < 0 for γ < γ_c and Λ_T(γ) > 0 for γ > γ_c. (2) Monotonicity: Λ_T(γ) is monotonically increasing in γ for fixed (L, D, T). (3) Agreement with KADR: |γ_c - γ_c^{KADR}| = O(1/N) where γ_c^{KADR} is the steepest-gradient locus of ρ̄(γ). (4) Finite-width crossover: the transition has width δγ ~ N^{-ν} with ν > 0, and ρ̄ ~ |γ - γ_c|^β with β = 1 at leading order. (5) Data dependence: γ_c depends on the data through {λ_k(Θ^{(0)})}, {⟨v_k, y⟩}, and the spectrum of H.
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (1): Continuity of Λ_T(γ). At γ = 0, δΘ is driven by r_0(t) through H but does not feed back, so Λ_T < 0 (residual decays, kernel perturbation bounded). At large γ, feedback amplification exceeds dissipation rate ηλ_min(Θ^{(0)}), yielding Λ_T > 0. Part (2): Ordering structure of Lyapunov exponents with respect to coupling strength (larger γ amplifies the H-tensor coupling). Parts (3)-(5): Relationship between the variational system and the full nonlinear ODE; Taylor expansion of ρ̄(γ) near γ_c.
theorems.enables: Theoretical justification that a phase boundary EXISTS and is well-defined. Without this theorem, the system is purely empirical curve-fitting. This theorem also justifies using KADR steepest-gradient as the operational boundary definition.

theorems.name: Theorem 5*: Perturbative Validity Bounds (Smooth Activations)
theorems.statement: Under Assumptions A1–A4, A7–A10, the 1-loop truncation satisfies ||Θ(t) - [Θ^{(0)}(t) + (1/N)Θ^{(1)}(t)]||_F ≤ C(L, σ) · exp(C'·γ·T) / N² for all t ∈ [0, T], where C and C' depend on the depth L and activation function bounds C_k from A8*. The bound provides a sufficient (not necessary) condition for the perturbative expansion to be accurate.
theorems.proof_strategy: Grönwall-type argument applied to the error of the 1-loop truncation. The nonlinear remainder couples through the H tensor with strength proportional to γ, leading to exponential growth. The bound is conservative (sufficient but not necessary); the computable diagnostic ε_1 provides a sharper practical indicator.
theorems.enables: Self-diagnostic validity indicators and minimum width determination for the perturbative regime. Establishes that perturbative validity is a well-defined concept even though the computable bound is exponential in T.

theorems.name: Lemma 3: Spatial Averaging Bound for Convolutional Kernels
theorems.statement: For a convolutional layer with spatial grid S and data with spatial correlation length ξ (defined as the decay length of the spatial autocorrelation of pre-activations): ||R_{ijk}||_F ≤ C · (ξ/√|S|) · ||H^{dense}||_F, where R is the remainder in the spatial decomposition (Theorem 2*). If ξ ≪ √|S|, the decomposition is accurate; if ξ ~ √|S|, long-range correlations prevent spatial averaging from improving the effective width.
theorems.proof_strategy: Model spatial contributions as a weakly correlated random field on the grid S. Apply a CLT for weakly dependent variables (mixing conditions), where the mixing rate is controlled by ξ. The variance of the sum scales as |S|·ξ² rather than |S|² (independent case), yielding the 1/√|S| concentration factor modulated by ξ.
theorems.enables: Quantifies when spatial averaging improves ConvNet predictions and when it fails (long-range correlated data). Needed for Theorem 2* error bound.

theorems.name: Structural Stability Lemma (Frozen-H)
theorems.statement: If ||H(t) - H(0)||_F / ||H(0)||_F ≤ ε for t ∈ [0, T], then the phase boundary predicted by the frozen-H ODE satisfies |γ_c^{frozen} - γ_c^{true}| ≤ C · ε · γ_c^{true}, where C is a constant depending on the spectral gap of Θ^{(0)}.
theorems.proof_strategy: Treat H(t) - H(0) as a perturbation to the frozen-H variational system. The Lyapunov exponent Λ_T is Lipschitz in H (with Lipschitz constant bounded by the training horizon T and the norm of r_0(t)), so a small perturbation to H produces a proportionally small shift in γ_c. Near the boundary (lazy regime), kernel drift is small by definition, so H(t) ≈ H(0) and ε is small. In the rich regime, the frozen-H ODE correctly predicts instability even if the quantitative trajectory diverges.
theorems.enables: Justifies the frozen-H approximation used in all predictions (Algorithm C*). Without this lemma, frozen-H is an uncontrolled approximation.

theorems.name: Conjecture 1: ReLU Extension
theorems.statement: Theorems 1*–5* hold for ReLU activation with the following modifications: Θ^{(0)} is replaced by the arc-cosine kernel, the C³ bounds on σ are replaced by distributional estimates, and the phase boundary location and scaling exponents are qualitatively identical to smooth activations.
theorems.proof_strategy: Unproven. Supported by empirical evidence from parallel GELU/ReLU experiments. The arc-cosine kernel formulas for infinite-width ReLU NTK provide a consistency check.
theorems.enables: Extending the framework to the most widely-used activation function. If confirmed empirically, this demonstrates universality of the phase structure across activation smoothness.

algorithms.name: NTK Computation
algorithms.purpose: Compute the exact Neural Tangent Kernel Θ_{ij} for a network of width N on dataset {x_1, …, x_n}, with per-layer decomposition
inputs: Network architecture f(x; θ) with parameter vector θ ∈ ℝ^P
Dataset X = {x_1, …, x_n}, each x_i ∈ ℝ^d
outputs: Per-layer decomposition {Θ^(ℓ)}_{ℓ=1}^L such that Θ = Σ_ℓ Θ^(ℓ)
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION ComputeNTK(f, X, θ):
    n ← |X|
    // Compute Jacobian J ∈ ℝ^{n × P} via vectorized autodiff
    J ← vmap(λ x: grad(f(x; θ), θ))(X)    // shape (n, P)
    
    // Per-layer NTK decomposition
    Θ ← zeros(n, n)
    Θ_layers ← []
    FOR ℓ = 1 TO L:
        J_ℓ ← J[:, param_slice(ℓ)]           // n × P_ℓ
        Θ_layers[ℓ] ← J_ℓ @ J_ℓ.T            // n × n
        Θ ← Θ + Θ_layers[ℓ]
    
    RETURN Θ, Θ_layers
algorithms.time_complexity: O(n · L · N² + n² · L · N²) — n backward passes each costing O(L·N²), then n² outer products over P = O(L·N²) parameters
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n · L · N² + n²) for the full Jacobian plus the kernel matrix
algorithms.correctness_argument: The NTK is defined as Θ_{ij} = J(θ)J(θ)^T. The algorithm computes J exactly via automatic differentiation (up to floating-point precision) and assembles the outer product. Per-layer decomposition follows from the additive structure of the Jacobian over parameter groups.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Use double precision for the Jacobian to avoid catastrophic cancellation in the outer product. For large P, consider chunked Jacobian computation to fit in memory. The vmap-based approach is 10-100x faster than sequential backward passes.

algorithms.name: Finite-Width Correction Extraction
algorithms.purpose: Extract the 1/N correction Θ^{(1)} from NTK matrices computed at multiple widths via weighted least-squares regression
inputs: Set of calibration widths {N_1, …, N_W} (e.g., {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}), W ≥ 3
For each width N_w: S independent NTK samples {Θ^{(s)}(N_w)}_{s=1}^S from random initialization
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION ExtractCorrection(widths, kernel_samples):
    W ← |widths|; n ← dim(kernel_samples[0][0])
    
    // Step 1: Average over seeds at each width
    FOR w = 1 TO W:
        Θ_mean[w] ← (1/S) Σ_{s=1}^S kernel_samples[w][s]
        Θ_var[w] ← (1/(S-1)) Σ_s (kernel_samples[w][s] - Θ_mean[w])²
    
    // Step 2: Centered WLS regression per entry
    // Model: Θ_mean(N) = Θ^{(0)} + (1/N) Θ^{(1)} + ε
    x_centered ← [1/N_w - mean(1/N_w) for w in 1..W]  // centered for stability
    FOR i = 1 TO n:
        FOR j = i TO n:    // exploit symmetry
            y ← [Θ_mean[w][i,j] for w in 1..W]
            x ← [1/widths[w] for w in 1..W]
            weights ← [S / Θ_var[w][i,j] for w in 1..W]  // inverse-variance
            A ← column_stack(ones(W), x)
            W_diag ← diag(weights)
            β ← solve(A^T W_diag A, A^T W_diag y)
            Θ0[i,j] = Θ0[j,i] = β[0]
            Θ1[i,j] = Θ1[j,i] = β[1]
            cov_β ← inv(A^T W_diag A)
            σ0[i,j] = sqrt(cov_β[0,0])
            σ1[i,j] = sqrt(cov_β[1,1])
    
    // Step 3: Validity check
    R ← sqrt(Σ_{i,j} residual²) / (n(n+1)/2)
    ratio ← max(|Θ1|) / max(|Θ0|)
    IF ratio > 0.5: WARN "Perturbative expansion may not converge"
    
    // Step 4: Two-loop convergence check (if W ≥ 4)
    IF W ≥ 4:
        // Fit 3-parameter model: Θ = Θ^{(0)} + (1/N)Θ^{(1)} + (1/N²)Θ^{(2)}
        // Compare Θ^{(0)}, Θ^{(1)} from 2-term vs 3-term fit
        // Report ε_3 = max relative difference
    
    RETURN Θ0, Θ1, R, σ0, σ1
algorithms.time_complexity: O(W · S · T_NTK + n² · W) where T_NTK is the cost of one NTK computation. NTK computations dominate; regression per entry is O(W).
algorithms.space_complexity: O(W · S · n² + n²) for storing all kernel samples plus outputs
algorithms.correctness_argument: The 1/N expansion (Def. 1.4) predicts Θ(N) = Θ^{(0)} + Θ^{(1)}/N + O(1/N²). WLS regression on the model y = β_0 + β_1·(1/N) recovers Θ^{(0)} = β_0 and Θ^{(1)} = β_1 with minimum-variance unbiased estimates under the assumed noise model. Centering reduces the condition number of A^TWA.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Use centered regression (fit on 1/N - mean(1/N)) to reduce condition number. Log-spaced widths {64, 128, 256, 512, 1024} spread 1/N values more uniformly. Report condition number of A^TWA; flag if > 10^4. Fall back to OLS if WLS condition number is too high.

algorithms.name: Kernel Evolution ODE Integration (Frozen-H with Periodic Recomputation)
algorithms.purpose: Solve the coupled kernel-residual ODE system with H frozen at initialization (primary mode) or periodically recomputed (validation mode)
inputs: Θ^{(0)}, Θ^{(1)}(0): initial kernel and correction
H_{ijk}: Hessian-Jacobian contraction tensor (frozen at t=0)
ODE method selector (RK45 or BDF for stiff systems)
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION SolveKernelODE(Θ0, Θ1, H, y, η, N, T, ε_tol):
    n ← dim(Θ0)
    Θ_init ← Θ0 + (1/N) * Θ1
    f_init ← zeros(n)          // f(x; θ_0) centered to zero
    r_init ← f_init - y
    z0 ← concatenate(upper_triangle(Θ_init), r_init)
    
    FUNCTION rhs(t, z):
        Θ ← unflatten_symmetric(z[0 : n(n+1)/2])
        r ← z[n(n+1)/2 : end]
        df_dt ← -η * Θ @ r                              // O(n²)
        dΘ_dt ← -(η/N) * einsum('ijk,k->ij', H, r)      // O(n³)
        dr_dt ← df_dt
        RETURN concatenate(upper_triangle(dΘ_dt), dr_dt)
    
    // Stiffness detection
    J_sample ← finite_diff_jacobian(rhs, 0, z0, δ=1e-7)
    λ_max ← max(abs(eigenvalues(J_sample)))
    IF λ_max * T > 1000:
        method ← "BDF"       // implicit for stiff systems
    ELSE:
        method ← "RK45"      // explicit adaptive
    
    // Integrate
    solution ← ode_solve(rhs, z0, t_span=(0, T),
        method=method, rtol=ε_tol, atol=ε_tol/100,
        max_step=T/10, dense_output=True)
    
    // Extract trajectory
    K ← ceil(T / Δt_output)
    t_points ← linspace(0, T, K)
    FOR k = 0 TO K:
        z_k ← solution(t_points[k])
        Θ_traj[k] ← unflatten_symmetric(z_k[0:n(n+1)/2])
        r_traj[k] ← z_k[n(n+1)/2:end]
    
    converged ← (norm(r_traj[-1]) < norm(r_traj[0]) * ε_tol)
    RETURN t_points, Θ_traj, r_traj, converged

// VALIDATION MODE: Periodically recompute H
FUNCTION SolveKernelODE_Validated(arch, X, Θ0, Θ1, H0, y, η, N, T, k_recomp=100):
    // Run frozen-H for k_recomp steps, then recompute H from updated θ
    θ ← initialize(arch, N)
    t = 0; Θ_traj_val = []; H_current = H0
    WHILE t < T:
        // Integrate for k_recomp steps with current H
        t_pts, Θ_seg, r_seg, _ ← SolveKernelODE(
            Θ_traj[-1] or Θ_init, Θ1, H_current, y, η, N,
            min(k_recomp*η, T-t), ε_tol)
        Θ_traj_val.extend(Θ_seg)
        // Update θ via gradient descent for k_recomp steps
        FOR step = 1 TO k_recomp:
            θ ← θ - η * grad(L(θ))
        // Recompute H from updated parameters
        H_current ← ComputeHTensor(arch, X, θ)
        t += k_recomp * η
    RETURN Θ_traj_val
algorithms.time_complexity: O(K_steps · n³) per RHS evaluation for the H-tensor contraction. For BDF: add O(n⁴) per step for Newton iteration on the implicit system. Total: O(K_steps · n³) with K_steps typically 100–10000.
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n³) for H tensor + O(n²) for state vector + O(K · n²) for stored trajectory
algorithms.correctness_argument: The ODE system directly implements Definition 1.6* (frozen-H kernel evolution). Correctness of the frozen-H approximation is bounded by the Structural Stability Lemma (SSL): if ||H(t) - H(0)||_F / ||H(0)||_F ≤ ε, the phase boundary error is O(ε·γ_c). Near the lazy-rich boundary (where predictions matter most), kernel drift is small by definition, so the approximation is self-consistently accurate.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Use adaptive step size control (RK45 with error estimation or BDF with Newton iteration). Detect stiffness via Jacobian eigenvalue estimate; switch to BDF if stiffness ratio > 1000. Store only upper triangle of Θ (symmetric). Use einsum for vectorized H-tensor contraction. For the validation mode, H recomputation every k=100 steps is O(n²·L·N²) per recomputation.

algorithms.name: Variational Lyapunov Exponent Computation (Corrected)
algorithms.purpose: Compute the finite-time Lyapunov exponent Λ_T(γ) of the variational system around the NTK trajectory, replacing the original steady-state eigenvalue tracking
inputs: NTK trajectory r_0(t) from frozen-kernel integration
outputs: Leading Lyapunov vector at time T
algorithms.time_complexity: O(K_steps · k · n³) where K_steps is the number of time steps and k is the number of Lyapunov vectors. The n³ comes from the H-tensor contraction in each variational RHS evaluation.
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n³ + D·k) for H tensor storage and the k Lyapunov vectors
algorithms.correctness_argument: The QR-based algorithm is the standard method for computing Lyapunov exponents of non-autonomous systems (Benettin et al., 1980). Periodic reorthogonalization prevents the k vectors from collapsing onto the most unstable direction. The finite-time Lyapunov exponent converges to the true maximal exponent as T → ∞ for ergodic systems; at finite T, it provides a well-defined stability measure. The variational system implements Definition 1.10* exactly: the non-zero coupling term -η δΘ · r_0(t) in the δr equation ensures genuine two-way feedback (fixing the F1 issue).
algorithms.numerical_considerations: The state-transition matrix Φ(T,0) can have exponentially large condition number, making direct computation infeasible. The QR-based algorithm avoids this by periodic reorthogonalization. Choose renorm_interval so that ||Q|| grows by at most ~10x between reorthogonalizations. If the variational system is stiff, use implicit integration (BDF) for each column. At very large γ, the exponent grows rapidly and fewer time steps may suffice.

algorithms.name: Stability Boundary Detection (Corrected)
algorithms.purpose: Find the critical coupling γ_c where the maximal Lyapunov exponent crosses zero: Λ_T(γ_c) = 0
inputs: Initial bracket [γ_lo, γ_hi] such that Λ_T(γ_lo) < 0 and Λ_T(γ_hi) > 0
outputs: Critical coupling γ_c to precision ε_bif
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION DetectStabilityBoundary(compute_lyapunov, γ_lo, γ_hi, ε_bif):
    // Step 1: Verify bracket
    Λ_lo ← compute_lyapunov(γ_lo)
    Λ_hi ← compute_lyapunov(γ_hi)
    ASSERT Λ_lo < 0 AND Λ_hi > 0, "Bracket does not contain a zero crossing"
    
    // Step 2: Bisection to narrow bracket
    WHILE (γ_hi - γ_lo) > 10 * ε_bif:
        γ_mid ← (γ_lo + γ_hi) / 2
        Λ_mid ← compute_lyapunov(γ_mid)
        IF Λ_mid < 0:
            γ_lo ← γ_mid; Λ_lo ← Λ_mid
        ELSE:
            γ_hi ← γ_mid; Λ_hi ← Λ_mid
    
    γ_c ← (γ_lo + γ_hi) / 2
    
    // Step 3: Refine with secant method
    FOR iter = 1 TO 5:
        γ_new ← γ_lo - Λ_lo * (γ_hi - γ_lo) / (Λ_hi - Λ_lo)
        γ_new ← clamp(γ_new, γ_lo, γ_hi)
        Λ_new ← compute_lyapunov(γ_new)
        IF abs(Λ_new) < ε_bif * abs(Λ_lo):
            γ_c ← γ_new; BREAK
        IF Λ_new < 0:
            γ_lo, Λ_lo ← γ_new, Λ_new
        ELSE:
            γ_hi, Λ_hi ← γ_new, Λ_new
    
    γ_c ← (γ_lo + γ_hi) / 2
    
    // Step 4: Compute full Lyapunov spectrum at γ_c
    Λ_T, Λ_spectrum, v_lead ← compute_lyapunov(γ_c, return_full=True)
    
    RETURN γ_c, Λ_spectrum, v_lead
algorithms.time_complexity: O(log((γ_hi - γ_lo)/ε_bif) · T_lyap) for bisection + O(1) secant refinements. T_lyap = O(K_steps · k · n³) from Algorithm D*. For ε_bif = 10^{-6}: ~20 bisection steps, total O(20 · K_steps · k · n³).
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n³) for H tensor storage (shared with Algorithm D*)
algorithms.correctness_argument: Theorem 4* part (2) guarantees that Λ_T(γ) is monotonically increasing in γ, so the zero-crossing is unique within any bracket. Bisection converges linearly; secant refinement achieves superlinear convergence. The bracket condition is verified before starting.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: The Lyapunov exponent computation (Algorithm D*) is the bottleneck. Each evaluation requires integrating the full variational system, so minimizing the number of evaluations is critical. The bisection + secant combination typically requires 20-25 total evaluations. If the Lyapunov exponent is very close to zero (flat region), increase the time horizon T to improve resolution.

algorithms.name: Pseudo-Arclength Continuation
algorithms.purpose: Track the phase boundary curve through multi-dimensional parameter space (γ vs. depth, data complexity, etc.) once an initial boundary point is found
inputs: Initial boundary point (γ_c, p_0) from Algorithm E*
Function G(γ, p) = Λ_T(γ, p) whose zero-set defines the boundary
outputs: Phase boundary curve {(γ_k, p_k)}_{k=0}^K as a sequence of points
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION PseudoArclengthContinuation(G, γ_0, p_0, Δs, s_max, bounds):
    curve ← [(γ_0, p_0)]
    s_total ← 0
    
    // Initial tangent direction
    δ ← 1e-4 * max(abs(γ_0), 1)
    ∂G_∂γ ← (G(γ_0+δ, p_0) - G(γ_0-δ, p_0)) / (2δ)
    ∂G_∂p ← (G(γ_0, p_0+δ) - G(γ_0, p_0-δ)) / (2δ)
    τ ← normalize((-∂G_∂p, ∂G_∂γ))
    IF τ[1] < 0: τ ← -τ    // orient toward increasing p
    
    prev_pt ← (γ_0, p_0); prev_τ ← τ
    
    WHILE s_total < s_max:
        // Predictor: Euler along tangent
        γ_pred ← prev_pt[0] + Δs * prev_τ[0]
        p_pred ← prev_pt[1] + Δs * prev_τ[1]
        γ_k, p_k ← γ_pred, p_pred
        
        // Corrector: Newton on augmented system
        FOR newton_iter = 1 TO 20:
            g ← G(γ_k, p_k)
            ∂g_∂γ ← (G(γ_k+δ, p_k) - G(γ_k-δ, p_k)) / (2δ)
            ∂g_∂p ← (G(γ_k, p_k+δ) - G(γ_k, p_k-δ)) / (2δ)
            h ← prev_τ[0]*(γ_k - γ_pred) + prev_τ[1]*(p_k - p_pred)
            
            J_aug ← [[∂g_∂γ, ∂g_∂p], [prev_τ[0], prev_τ[1]]]
            IF abs(det(J_aug)) < 1e-14:
                WARN "Possible fold point"; Δs ← Δs/2; BREAK
            
            Δγ, Δp ← solve(J_aug, [-g, -h])
            γ_k += Δγ; p_k += Δp
            IF sqrt(Δγ² + Δp²) < 1e-10: BREAK
        
        IF abs(G(γ_k, p_k)) > 1e-6:
            Δs ← Δs/2
            IF Δs < 1e-8: WARN "Stalled"; BREAK
            CONTINUE
        IF NOT in_bounds((γ_k, p_k), bounds): BREAK
        
        // Update tangent with adaptive step size
        ∂G_∂γ ← (G(γ_k+δ, p_k) - G(γ_k-δ, p_k)) / (2δ)
        ∂G_∂p ← (G(γ_k, p_k+δ) - G(γ_k, p_k-δ)) / (2δ)
        τ_new ← normalize((-∂G_∂p, ∂G_∂γ))
        IF dot(τ_new, prev_τ) < 0: τ_new ← -τ_new
        
        cos_angle ← dot(τ_new, prev_τ)
        IF cos_angle < 0.5: Δs ← max(Δs*0.5, Δs_min)
        ELIF cos_angle > 0.95: Δs ← min(Δs*1.5, Δs_max)
        
        curve.append((γ_k, p_k))
        s_total += Δs
        prev_pt ← (γ_k, p_k); prev_τ ← τ_new
    
    RETURN curve
algorithms.time_complexity: O(K_steps · n_newton · T_G) where T_G is the cost of evaluating G (one Lyapunov exponent ≈ O(K_ode · k · n³)), n_newton ≤ 20. Gradient evaluations add 2 extra G evaluations per Newton step. Total: O(K_cont · 60 · K_ode · k · n³).
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n³ + K_cont) for H tensor and curve storage
algorithms.correctness_argument: Pseudo-arclength continuation is a standard method for tracking implicitly-defined curves through parameter space (Keller, 1977). The augmented Newton system has a unique solution as long as the Jacobian is non-singular (no fold points). Adaptive step size control based on curvature ensures the predictor stays close to the curve.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Fold points (where the boundary curve turns back) are detected by a singular augmented Jacobian. At fold points, reduce step size and attempt to continue. Cusps and branch points require special handling (not implemented; flagged as warnings). Finite-difference gradients use centered differences with step size δ = 10^{-4} · max(|γ|, 1).

algorithms.name: Nyström Approximation
algorithms.purpose: Approximate the n×n kernel matrix using m ≪ n landmark points for scaling experiments with n > 1000
outputs: Low-rank factor L ∈ ℝ^{n×r} such that Θ̃ = LL^T
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION NystromApproximate(X, kernel_fn, m, strategy="leverage"):
    n ← |X|
    
    // Landmark selection
    IF strategy == "uniform":
        landmarks ← random_sample(range(n), m)
    ELIF strategy == "kmeans":
        centers, landmarks ← kmeans_pp(X, m)
    ELIF strategy == "leverage":
        m_pilot ← min(2*m, n)
        pilot ← random_sample(range(n), m_pilot)
        K_pilot ← kernel_fn(X[pilot], X[pilot])
        U, Σ, _ ← svd(K_pilot)
        K_cross ← kernel_fn(X, X[pilot])
        approx_embed ← K_cross @ U[:,:m] @ diag(1/Σ[:m])
        scores ← row_norms(approx_embed)²
        scores ← scores / sum(scores)
        landmarks ← weighted_sample(range(n), m, weights=scores)
    
    // Compute kernel sub-matrices
    K_mm ← kernel_fn(X[landmarks], X[landmarks])    // m×m
    K_nm ← kernel_fn(X, X[landmarks])                // n×m
    
    // Stabilized factorization
    U, Σ, Vt ← svd(K_mm)
    σ_threshold ← max(Σ) * 1e-10
    r ← count(Σ > σ_threshold)
    L ← K_nm @ U[:,:r] @ diag(1/sqrt(Σ[:r]))    // n×r, Θ̃ = LL^T
    
    // Error estimation
    n_check ← min(100, n)
    idx ← random_sample(range(n), n_check)
    Θ_exact ← kernel_fn(X[idx], X[idx])
    Θ_approx ← L[idx] @ L[idx].T
    ε_nys ← norm(Θ_exact - Θ_approx, 'fro') / norm(Θ_exact, 'fro')
    
    RETURN L, landmarks, ε_nys, r

FUNCTION NystromSolve(L, b, λ_reg):
    // Solve (LL^T + λI)x = b via Woodbury identity
    r ← L.shape[1]
    LtL ← L.T @ L                           // r×r
    inner ← LtL + λ_reg * eye(r)             // r×r
    x ← (1/λ_reg) * (b - L @ solve(inner, L.T @ b))
    RETURN x
algorithms.time_complexity: O(n · m · T_kernel + m³) for kernel sub-matrix computation and SVD. NystromSolve: O(n·r + r³) per solve.
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n·r + m²) for L and K_mm, replacing O(n²) for the full kernel
algorithms.correctness_argument: The Nyström approximation Θ̃ = K_nm K_mm^{-1} K_mn is the optimal rank-m approximation in the RKHS norm (Gittens & Mahoney, 2016). The factored form L = K_nm U diag(1/√Σ) satisfies Θ̃ = LL^T exactly. The Woodbury identity for NystromSolve is algebraically exact.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Truncate small singular values of K_mm to avoid numerical instability (threshold at max(Σ) · 10^{-10}). Leverage-score sampling provides better approximation quality than uniform sampling for ill-conditioned kernels but has higher setup cost. At n_probe = 200 (primary experiments), Nyström is NOT used; it is reserved for scaling experiments with n > 1000.

algorithms.name: Phase Diagram Sweep (End-to-End Pipeline)
algorithms.purpose: Orchestrate the full pipeline over a grid of hyperparameters to produce a phase diagram with regime labels, boundary curves, order parameters, and confidence levels
inputs: Architecture specification (layer types, activation, skip connections)
outputs: Phase diagram: regime label (lazy/rich/chaotic/divergent) at each grid point
Confidence level at each grid point
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION PhaseDiagramSweep(arch, X, y, η_grid, N_grid, params):
    n ← |X|
    
    // ===== STAGE 1: Calibration (amortized) =====
    cal_widths ← select_calibration_widths(N_grid)  // e.g. {64,128,256,512,1024}
    S ← 10  // seeds per width
    
    kernel_cache ← {}
    FOR N_cal IN cal_widths:           // PARALLELIZABLE
        FOR seed = 1 TO S:             // PARALLELIZABLE
            θ ← initialize(arch, N_cal, seed)
            Θ, Θ_layers ← ComputeNTK(arch, X, θ)    // ALG-A
            kernel_cache[N_cal].append(Θ)
    
    Θ0, Θ1, R, σ0, σ1 ← ExtractCorrection(cal_widths, kernel_cache)  // ALG-B
    H ← ComputeHTensor(arch, X, cal_widths, kernel_cache)
    
    // ===== STAGE 2: Per-Grid-Point Prediction =====
    results ← empty_grid(|η_grid|, |N_grid|)
    
    FOR N IN N_grid:                    // PARALLELIZABLE
        FOR η IN η_grid:                // PARALLELIZABLE
            a, b ← get_muP_exponents(arch)
            γ ← η * N^(-(1-a-b)) * ||H||_F / ||Θ0||_F
            
            // Kernel ODE
            t_pts, Θ_traj, r_traj, conv ← SolveKernelODE(
                Θ0, Θ1, H, y, η, N, T_max, ε_tol)       // ALG-C
            
            // Lyapunov exponent
            Λ_T, Λ_spec, v_lead ← ComputeLyapunovExponent(
                r_traj, t_pts, Θ0, H, γ, η, T_max, k=10)  // ALG-D*
            
            // Regime classification
            IF NOT conv AND norm(r_traj[-1]) > 2*norm(r_traj[0]):
                regime ← "divergent"
            ELIF Λ_T < -ε_stable:
                regime ← "lazy"
            ELIF Λ_T > ε_stable:
                IF abs(Im(Λ_spec[0])) > ε_osc:
                    regime ← "chaotic"
                ELSE:
                    regime ← "rich"
            ELSE:
                regime ← "boundary"
            
            // Order parameter (KADR)
            kadr ← ComputeOrderParameter(Θ_traj, Θ0, t_pts)  // ALG-J
            
            // Confidence
            conf ← ClassifyConfidence(
                Θ0, Θ1, N, R, σ0, σ1, Λ_spec)              // ALG-I
            
            results[N, η] ← {regime, γ, Λ_T, kadr, conf, Λ_spec}
    
    // ===== STAGE 3: Boundary Refinement =====
    boundary_candidates ← []
    FOR each adjacent pair (p1, p2) in grid:
        IF results[p1].regime ≠ results[p2].regime:
            boundary_candidates.append((p1, p2))
    
    phase_boundaries ← []
    FOR (p1, p2) IN boundary_candidates:
        γ_c, Λ_spec, v ← DetectStabilityBoundary(
            compute_lyap_at, p1.γ, p2.γ, ε_bif)               // ALG-E*
        
        IF should_continue(γ_c, phase_boundaries):
            curve ← PseudoArclengthContinuation(
                G, γ_c, p_secondary, Δs, s_max, bounds)       // ALG-F
            phase_boundaries.append(curve)
    
    RETURN results, phase_boundaries
algorithms.time_complexity: Stage 1 (one-time): O(|cal_widths| · S · n² · L · N_max²). Stage 2 (per grid point): O(K_ode · n³ + K_ode · k · n³) for ODE + Lyapunov. Over full grid: O(|grid| · K_ode · k · n³). Stage 3: O(|boundaries| · K_cont · 60 · K_ode · k · n³). With n=200, k=10, K_ode=500, |grid|=200: feasible on laptop in ~1-2 hours.
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n³) for H tensor (dominant), shared across all grid points. O(|grid| · n²) for results if trajectories stored.
algorithms.correctness_argument: The pipeline implements the full theory: 1/N expansion (ALG-B) → kernel ODE (ALG-C) → Lyapunov stability (ALG-D*) → boundary detection (ALG-E*) → continuation (ALG-F). Each stage's correctness follows from the corresponding theorem. The end-to-end prediction is that the KADR steepest-gradient locus (ALG-J) agrees with the Lyapunov zero-crossing (ALG-E*) to O(1/N) (Theorem 4* part 3).
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Parallelization across grid points in Stage 2 provides near-linear speedup. Cache Θ0, Θ1, H across grid points (they depend only on architecture, not on η or N_target). Adaptive grid refinement near boundaries improves resolution without increasing total grid size. For very large grids, use coarse scan + adaptive refinement strategy.

algorithms.name: Confidence Stratification
algorithms.purpose: Classify each prediction into confidence tiers based on multiple validity indicators, providing self-diagnostic uncertainty quantification
inputs: Θ^{(0)}, Θ^{(1)}: infinite-width kernel and correction
Fit residual R, standard errors σ0, σ1
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION ClassifyConfidence(Θ0, Θ1, N, R, σ0, σ1, Λ_spectrum,
                           ε_nys=None, κ4_mag=None):
    flags ← []; scores ← {}
    
    // Indicator 1: Perturbative validity
    ε_1 ← norm(Θ1, 'fro') / (N * norm(Θ0, 'fro'))
    scores['perturbative_ratio'] ← ε_1
    IF ε_1 > 0.5: flags.append("PERTURBATIVE_BREAKDOWN")
    ELIF ε_1 > 0.2: flags.append("PERTURBATIVE_WARNING")
    
    // Indicator 2: Regression quality
    scores['fit_residual'] ← R
    IF R > 0.1: flags.append("POOR_FIT")
    
    // Indicator 3: Lyapunov exponent gap
    Λ_max ← Λ_spectrum[0]
    Λ_scale ← max(abs(Λ_spectrum))
    gap ← abs(Λ_max) / max(Λ_scale, 1e-10)
    scores['lyapunov_gap'] ← gap
    IF gap < 0.01: flags.append("NEAR_BOUNDARY")
    
    // Indicator 4: Kernel condition number
    κ ← condition_number(Θ0)
    scores['kernel_condition'] ← κ
    IF κ > 1e10: flags.append("ILL_CONDITIONED")
    ELIF κ > 1e6: flags.append("MODERATE_CONDITIONING")
    
    // Indicator 5: Nyström error
    IF ε_nys IS NOT None:
        scores['nystrom_error'] ← ε_nys
        IF ε_nys > 0.1: flags.append("HIGH_NYSTROM_ERROR")
    
    // Indicator 6: Moment closure quality
    IF κ4_mag IS NOT None:
        scores['kappa4_magnitude'] ← κ4_mag
        IF κ4_mag > 0.3: flags.append("MOMENT_CLOSURE_SUSPECT")
    
    // Indicator 7: Gradient flow assumption
    // (passed in externally based on η value)
    
    // Aggregate into tiers
    critical ← {"PERTURBATIVE_BREAKDOWN", "ILL_CONDITIONED", "POOR_FIT"}
    warning ← {"PERTURBATIVE_WARNING", "NEAR_BOUNDARY",
               "MODERATE_CONDITIONING", "HIGH_NYSTROM_ERROR",
               "MOMENT_CLOSURE_SUSPECT"}
    
    n_crit ← count(flags ∩ critical)
    n_warn ← count(flags ∩ warning)
    
    IF n_crit > 0 OR n_warn >= 2: tier ← "low"
    ELIF n_warn == 1: tier ← "moderate"
    ELSE: tier ← "high"
    
    RETURN tier, scores, flags
algorithms.time_complexity: O(n²) dominated by condition number computation (SVD of Θ^{(0)})
algorithms.space_complexity: O(n²) for the kernel matrix (already in memory)
algorithms.correctness_argument: Each indicator corresponds to a specific assumption or approximation in the theory: ε_1 monitors A5 (expansion convergence), R monitors the regression quality, the Lyapunov gap indicates proximity to the phase boundary (where predictions are inherently less certain), and κ monitors A9 (data regularity). The tier aggregation is conservative: any critical flag triggers low confidence.
algorithms.numerical_considerations: The condition number computation uses SVD, which is O(n³) but n = 200 makes this fast. The thresholds (0.2, 0.5, 0.1, 10^6, 10^10) are calibrated from pilot experiments and may require adjustment for specific architecture families.

algorithms.name: Order Parameter Computation (KADR)
algorithms.purpose: Compute the Kernel Alignment Drift Rate — the primary order parameter distinguishing lazy and rich regimes
outputs: KADR drift rate ρ̄ (scalar order parameter)
algorithms.pseudocode:
FUNCTION ComputeOrderParameter(Θ_traj, Θ0, t_points):
    K ← len(Θ_traj); n ← dim(Θ0)
    Θ0_norm ← norm(Θ0, 'fro')
    
    // Kernel alignment trajectory
    alignment ← zeros(K)
    FOR k = 0 TO K-1:
        alignment[k] ← trace(Θ_traj[k].T @ Θ0) / (norm(Θ_traj[k], 'fro') * Θ0_norm)
    
    // Normalized Frobenius distance (KADR trajectory)
    distance ← zeros(K)
    FOR k = 0 TO K-1:
        distance[k] ← norm(Θ_traj[k] - Θ0, 'fro') / Θ0_norm
    
    // Spectral distance (top eigenvalue displacement)
    λ0 ← eigenvalues(Θ0, top_k=10)
    spectral_dist ← zeros(K)
    FOR k = 0 TO K-1:
        λ_k ← eigenvalues(Θ_traj[k], top_k=10)
        spectral_dist[k] ← norm(λ_k - λ0) / norm(λ0)
    
    // Time-averaged KADR
    ρ_bar ← trapezoid_integrate(distance, t_points) / t_points[-1]
    
    // Drift rate via linear regression on early trajectory
    T_fit ← t_points[-1] / 4
    mask ← [k for k in range(K) if t_points[k] ≤ T_fit]
    α, _ ← linear_regression(t_points[mask], distance[mask])
    
    // Steepest gradient detection (for boundary localization)
    // Used when sweeping over γ: find γ where dρ̄/dγ is maximal
    d_final ← distance[-1]
    
    // Classification
    IF α < 0.01 AND d_final < 0.05:
        classification ← "lazy"
    ELIF d_final > 0.5:
        classification ← "rich"
    ELSE:
        classification ← "transitional"
    
    RETURN {
        kadr_mean: ρ_bar,
        drift_rate: α,
        final_displacement: d_final,
        alignment_trajectory: alignment,
        distance_trajectory: distance,
        spectral_distance: spectral_dist,
        classification: classification
    }
algorithms.time_complexity: O(K · n²) for Frobenius computations + O(K · n² · k_spec) for spectral distances. With K ~ 500 and n = 200: ~seconds.
algorithms.space_complexity: O(K · n²) if full trajectory stored; reducible to O(n²) by streaming
algorithms.correctness_argument: The KADR (Definition 1.9) measures kernel drift from the infinite-width reference. In the lazy regime, Θ(t) ≈ Θ^{(0)} so ρ̄ ≈ 0. In the rich regime, the kernel evolves significantly and ρ̄ ≫ 0. The steepest-gradient locus of ρ̄(γ) defines the phase boundary (Definition 1.10a). Theorem 4* part (3) guarantees agreement with the Lyapunov stability boundary to O(1/N).
algorithms.numerical_considerations: Use the time-averaged KADR ρ̄ rather than the instantaneous drift rate for robustness to transient oscillations. The spectral distance provides a complementary view but is more expensive (eigendecomposition at each time step). The linear regression for drift rate uses only the first quarter of the trajectory to avoid contamination from nonlinear saturation effects.

complexity_analysis.end_to_end: Total: O(W·S·n²·L·N_max² + |grid|·K_ode·k·n³ + |boundaries|·K_cont·60·K_ode·k·n³). With typical values n=200, L=4, N_max=1024, W=5, S=10, |grid|=200, K_ode=500, k=10: Stage 1 (calibration) ≈ 5·10·40000·4·10^6 ≈ 8×10^12 FLOPs (~minutes with vectorized autodiff). Stage 2 (predictions) ≈ 200·500·10·8×10^6 ≈ 8×10^12 FLOPs (~minutes). Stage 3 (boundary refinement) ≈ 10·50·60·500·10·8×10^6 ≈ 1.2×10^13 FLOPs (~minutes). Total: ~1–2 hours on laptop CPU.
complexity_analysis.bottleneck: The n³ scaling of the H-tensor contraction in the kernel ODE RHS (Algorithm C) is the fundamental per-step bottleneck. For the full pipeline, the Lyapunov exponent computation (Algorithm D*) dominates because it requires integrating k variational vectors through K_ode time steps, each with an O(n³) RHS. Reducing n via Nyström is essential for n > 1000.
complexity_analysis.feasibility_on_laptop: YES for n_probe = 200, depths 2–6, widths 64–1024. System prediction time ≈ 2–20 minutes per architecture-dataset pair. Full phase diagram sweep (200 grid points) ≈ 1–2 hours. The expensive part is GROUND TRUTH computation (full training runs for validation), requiring ~3050 CPU-hours total across all experiments — this needs a compute cluster or cloud, not the system prediction itself.
complexity_analysis.scaling_limits: n > 1000: requires Nyström (Algorithm G). n > 5000: Nyström error may become O(γ_c), limiting accuracy. N > 2048: NTK computation becomes expensive (O(n·L·N²)); may need stochastic estimation. Depth L > 10: perturbative expansion convergence degrades; ε_3 check may fail. Width < 64: 1/N correction is too large for perturbative regime.

modules.name: kernel_engine
modules.purpose: NTK computation, per-layer decomposition, and finite-width correction extraction (Algorithms A and B)

modules.name: h_tensor
modules.purpose: Compute and store the Hessian-Jacobian contraction tensor H_{ijk} for MLP, Conv, and ResNet architectures (Definitions 1.7a–c)

modules.name: dynamics
modules.purpose: Kernel evolution ODE integration (frozen-H and validated modes), Lyapunov exponent computation, and stability boundary detection (Algorithms C, D*, E*)

modules.name: phase_boundary
modules.purpose: Phase boundary detection, pseudo-arclength continuation, and boundary curve tracing (Algorithms E*, F)

modules.name: order_parameter
modules.purpose: KADR computation, regime classification, and steepest-gradient boundary localization (Algorithm J)

modules.name: confidence
modules.purpose: Perturbative validity indicators, confidence tier classification, and self-diagnostic reporting (Algorithm I)

modules.name: nystrom
modules.purpose: Nyström kernel approximation for scaling experiments (Algorithm G)

modules.name: pipeline
modules.purpose: End-to-end phase diagram sweep orchestration, grid management, caching, and parallelization (Algorithm H)

modules.name: ground_truth
modules.purpose: Full training runs for ground truth generation, multi-indicator measurement (KADR, CKA, weight displacement, linear probe gap), and experimental validation

modules.name: visualization
modules.purpose: Phase diagram plotting, boundary curves, scaling plots, and paper-quality figures

implementation_strategy.data_flow: kernel_engine computes NTK at calibration widths → h_tensor extracts H_{ijk} → dynamics integrates kernel ODE and computes Lyapunov exponents → order_parameter computes KADR from ODE trajectories → phase_boundary detects and traces boundaries → confidence classifies prediction quality → pipeline orchestrates the full sweep across the hyperparameter grid. Ground truth module runs independently to generate validation data. All modules share Θ^{(0)}, Θ^{(1)}, and H as cached intermediate results.
implementation_strategy.parallelization: Stage 1 (calibration): NTK computations at different widths and seeds are embarrassingly parallel. Stage 2 (grid sweep): each (η, N) grid point is independent; embarrassingly parallel across grid points. Stage 3 (boundary refinement): continuation along different boundary segments can be parallelized. Use joblib.Parallel with n_jobs=-1 for CPU parallelism. JAX's vmap provides vectorization within each NTK computation.
implementation_strategy.caching_strategy: Cache Θ^{(0)}, Θ^{(1)}, H per architecture-dataset pair (these are independent of target width and learning rate). Cache NTK samples at calibration widths to disk (HDF5 format). Cache kernel ODE trajectories for grid points near boundaries (for refinement). Invalidate cache if architecture or dataset changes. Estimated cache size: ~500MB per architecture-dataset pair.

primary_metrics: AUC (Area Under ROC Curve) for binary regime classification (lazy vs. rich) at each (η, N) grid point
Phase boundary localization error: |γ_c^{predicted} - γ_c^{measured}| / γ_c^{measured}
KADR prediction correlation: Pearson r between predicted ρ̄ and measured ρ̄ across grid points
Margin over baselines: AUC_system - AUC_baseline for each baseline
Confidence calibration: fraction of high-confidence predictions that are correct
baselines: Baseline A (Infinite-Width): Predict lazy for all configurations (the NTK limit prediction)
Baseline B (Naive γ Threshold): Classify based on γ > γ_threshold with threshold optimized on training data
Baseline C (Empirical KADR at Single Width): Measure KADR at target width directly (no theory, just measurement)
Baseline D (Random Forest on Architecture Features): Train RF classifier on (depth, width, η, activation) features using ground truth labels

Baseline E (Width Interpolation — Strengthened): Given KADR at 5 calibration widths, fit KADR(N) = a + b/N via linear regression and predict at target width. Uses same 5 widths and 10 seeds as system.
ablations: Remove 1/N correction (use Θ^{(0)} only): isolates the contribution of the perturbative expansion
Remove H-tensor (no kernel evolution): isolates the contribution of the kernel ODE dynamics
Replace Lyapunov analysis with fixed-point eigenvalue analysis (original formulation): tests whether the corrected formulation matters
Replace GELU with ReLU (Conjecture 1 test): tests activation robustness
Replace frozen-H with periodically recomputed H: tests the frozen-H approximation quality
Remove confidence stratification: tests whether UQ adds value (does filtering to high-confidence improve AUC?)
Vary n_probe (100, 200, 500): tests sensitivity to probe set size
Vary number of calibration widths (3, 4, 5): tests regression stability
retrodiction_targets: Chizat & Bach (2019): In the lazy regime (γ → 0), the system should predict zero kernel drift (KADR = 0) and recovery of the NTK training dynamics
Saxe et al. (2014): For deep linear networks, the phase boundary should coincide with the known critical learning rate for linear dynamics
Yang & Hu (2021) μP limits: Under μP parameterization, the system should predict rich regime at all widths (γ grows with N), consistent with the μP design principle
Lee et al. (2020) NTK vs. finite-width: The system should reproduce the known finding that narrow networks deviate from NTK predictions more than wide ones
novel_predictions: Finite-size scaling: Phase boundary sharpness Δη/η* ~ N^{-β} with β > 0. This connects neural network training to finite-size scaling in statistical mechanics.
Architecture-dependent critical exponents: Different architecture families (MLP vs. ConvNet vs. ResNet) may have different β exponents, indicating universality classes in training dynamics.
Dataset complexity dependence: γ_c = f(d_eff) where d_eff is the effective dimensionality of the target function, with predicted scaling η* ~ d_eff^{-α}.
Depth-dependent boundary shift: η*(L) ~ L^{-δ} with δ > 0, computable from Theorem 3* (ResNet H-tensor composition).
Spatial averaging in ConvNets: ConvNets have effective width N_eff ~ N · |S|, shifting the phase boundary relative to MLPs with the same parameter count.
evaluation_strategy.failure_criteria: Paper rejection if: (1) AUC < 0.85 on majority of architecture families (system does not beat random + baselines meaningfully); (2) Phase boundary localization error > 30% relative on calibration architectures; (3) Definition concordance fails (>10% disagreement on Exp 0); (4) Known limits (Chizat & Bach, μP) not recovered by retrodictions; (5) No sharpening with width (β = 0, no finite-size scaling); (6) Baseline E matches system accuracy (contribution reduces to theoretical framework only, which may not be sufficient for a top venue).

risk_assessment.risk: Lyapunov exponent computation is numerically unstable for the non-autonomous variational system (exponentially growing condition number of state-transition matrix)
risk_assessment.mitigation: Use QR-based Lyapunov exponent computation (Benettin et al., 1980) with periodic reorthogonalization. This is the standard method in dynamical systems and avoids the exponential condition number. If QR-based method fails, fall back to direct KADR prediction from ODE trajectory (bypass Lyapunov analysis entirely; lose theoretical justification but retain operational predictions).

risk_assessment.risk: Frozen-H approximation fails near the phase boundary where kernel drift is nontrivial
risk_assessment.mitigation: Experiment 1 (frozen-H validation) catches this early by comparing frozen-H and periodically-recomputed-H boundaries on calibration architectures. If boundary shift > 15%, use periodic H recomputation (expensive but feasible for a subset of configurations). The structural stability lemma bounds the error; if the constant C is large, report as a limitation.

risk_assessment.risk: AUC < 0.85 for ConvNet architectures due to stacked assumption violations (A4, spatial structure, non-uniform widths)
risk_assessment.mitigation: Present MLP results as primary validation, ConvNets as extension. If ConvNets fail, the novel H-tensor math (Theorems 2*, 3*) is unvalidated but the paper can stand on MLP results + mathematical contribution. Add native-resolution ConvNet experiments (S5 mitigation) to diagnose whether the issue is PCA preprocessing or fundamental theory failure.

risk_assessment.risk: Strengthened Baseline E (5 widths, 10 seeds, linear interpolation) matches system accuracy, reducing contribution to theoretical framework only
risk_assessment.mitigation: Reframe contribution: system predicts WITHOUT any training runs at target width (given calibration), while Baseline E requires training at 5 widths per target configuration. Emphasize the theoretical framework as the primary contribution. Also: system provides phase boundary curves (not just point predictions), scaling exponents, and universality analysis that Baseline E cannot provide.

risk_assessment.risk: Two-loop check (ε_3) fails at widths ≤ 256, restricting experimental matrix to N ≥ 512
risk_assessment.mitigation: Report minimum reliable width per architecture. If N_min ≥ 512, the paper still demonstrates the approach at larger widths. The finite-width phase diagram is still novel even if restricted to N ∈ {512, 1024}. Consider adding N = 2048 to compensate.

risk_assessment.risk: Definition concordance experiment (Exp 0) fails: Lyapunov, KADR-gradient, and sigmoid inflection definitions disagree by > 10%
risk_assessment.mitigation: Use only steepest-gradient of KADR (most robust definition). Acknowledge that eigenvalue-based theory provides motivation but not operationally equivalent definition. The paper's predictions still work via KADR; the theoretical justification is weakened but not invalidated.

risk_assessment.risk: GELU and ReLU phase structures qualitatively differ, undermining universality claims
risk_assessment.mitigation: Report as a discovery: activation smoothness matters for phase structure. The paper's theoretical contribution (for smooth activations) remains valid. Conjecture 1 is falsified, which is itself an interesting result.

risk_assessment.risk: Ground truth is ambiguous: > 30% of configurations have split-vote across 4 indicators
risk_assessment.mitigation: Reduce to 2-indicator conjunction (KADR + CKA) or treat regime as a continuous variable (predict ρ̄ rather than binary classification). Report per-indicator AUC separately.

risk_assessment.risk: Moment closure (κ_4 = 0) introduces significant systematic bias even for GELU at practical widths
risk_assessment.mitigation: Compute κ_4/κ_2² at initialization from Monte Carlo for each architecture. Report values and discuss systematic bias. With GELU, κ_4 is expected to be small; if surprisingly large, include as a source of error in the uncertainty budget.

risk_assessment.risk: Compute budget exceeded (> 4000 CPU-hours needed for full evaluation)
risk_assessment.mitigation: Drop initialization scale factor (3 → 1 value) and width-1024 experiments if budget is tight. Prioritize MLP experiments, GELU activation, and the core 3 architecture families. Defer ConvNet native-resolution experiments to revision.

risk_assessment.risk: Arnoldi eigensolver fails for near-degenerate Lyapunov exponents
risk_assessment.mitigation: Increase Arnoldi iteration count and Krylov subspace dimension. Use shift-invert mode for clustered eigenvalues. Fall back to full eigendecomposition for small systems (n < 100).

risk_assessment.risk: Pseudo-arclength continuation encounters cusps or branch points in the phase boundary curve
risk_assessment.mitigation: Detect via singular augmented Jacobian. Reduce step size and attempt to bypass. For branch points, choose the physically relevant branch (continuation along the lazy-rich boundary, not secondary instabilities).

risk_assessment.risk: Retrodiction experiments fail: Chizat & Bach limit or μP limits not recovered
risk_assessment.mitigation: These are sanity checks; failure indicates a fundamental bug in the implementation rather than a theory problem. Debug by checking infinite-width limit (should recover NTK dynamics exactly) and μP limit (should recover feature learning at all widths). If the theory itself is wrong, the paper cannot proceed.

new_mathematics: Theorem 2*: H_{ijk} decomposition for convolutional layers with weight sharing — no prior work computes the Hessian-Jacobian contraction tensor for weight-shared architectures. The spatial averaging factor 1/|S| and the correlation-length correction are new.
Theorem 3*: H_{ijk} composition rule for skip connections — the cross-term structure Ξ_ℓ between skip and branch paths, and the O(1/L) per-block depth scaling, are new mathematical results.
Lemma 3: Spatial averaging bound — quantifies when convolutional weight sharing provides statistical benefits for the H-tensor decomposition, via a CLT for weakly dependent spatial contributions.

Definition 1.10* / Theorem 4*: Lyapunov stability formulation of the phase boundary — reformulates the lazy-to-rich transition as a parametric stability boundary of the NTK trajectory using finite-time Lyapunov exponents. Previous work (Chizat & Bach, Yang & Hu) implies but never formalizes this as a trajectory stability boundary. The corrected formulation is a stronger contribution than the original steady-state bifurcation analysis.

Structural Stability Lemma: Bounds the error of the frozen-H approximation on the phase boundary location. New result establishing that the phase boundary is more robust than the full trajectory to H-tensor perturbations.
new_algorithms: Algorithm D*: Variational Lyapunov exponent computation for NTK dynamics — QR-based finite-time Lyapunov exponent computation applied to the non-autonomous variational system around the NTK trajectory. Standard in dynamical systems; new application to neural network training dynamics.

Algorithm E*: Stability boundary detection via bisection/secant on the Lyapunov exponent zero-crossing — replaces the original eigenvalue-based bifurcation detection with a theoretically sound Lyapunov-based approach.

Algorithm H: End-to-end phase diagram sweep pipeline — individual components are standard; the integration of 1/N expansion, kernel ODE, Lyapunov stability, continuation, and confidence stratification into a single predictive system is new.

Algorithm I: Confidence stratification with calibrated multi-indicator thresholds — self-diagnostic system that flags unreliable predictions based on perturbative validity, regression quality, Lyapunov gap, and kernel conditioning.
Frozen-H with structural stability validation protocol — principled approximation strategy with theoretical bounds and empirical validation experiment.
new_methodology: 4-indicator ground truth conjunction: KADR + CKA + weight displacement + linear probe gap with majority vote for robust regime classification
Definition concordance experiment: systematic comparison of three definitions of the phase boundary (Lyapunov zero-crossing, KADR steepest-gradient, sigmoid inflection) on calibration architectures
Frozen-H validation experiment: comparison of frozen-H and periodically-recomputed-H phase boundary predictions to quantify approximation error
ReLU/GELU comparison as activation robustness test: parallel experiments testing universality of phase structure across activation smoothness
Adaptive boundary refinement protocol: data-driven coarse → refine → augment pipeline for efficient phase diagram construction
Two-loop convergence diagnostic (ε_3): empirical check on 1/N expansion reliability via 3-parameter regression at calibration widths
new_predictions: Phase boundary sharpness scales as Δη/η* ~ N^{-β} with β > 0, connecting neural network training to finite-size scaling in statistical mechanics. Primary novel prediction; best-paper-level if confirmed at p < 0.01.
Architecture-dependent critical exponents: different architecture families (MLP, ConvNet, ResNet) may belong to different universality classes characterized by distinct β exponents.
Dataset complexity determines boundary location: γ_c = f(d_eff) with quantitative scaling η* ~ d_eff^{-α}.
Depth-dependent boundary shift: η*(L) ~ L^{-δ} with δ > 0, computable from Theorem 3* (ResNet H-tensor composition rule).
Spatial averaging improves effective width for ConvNets: N_eff ~ N · |S|, shifting the phase boundary relative to MLPs with the same channel count.
